retrieved_docs.title: Suicidal Ideation -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Suicide arises from a variety of risk factors that build up throughout life, with individual psychiatric disorders exerting the most significant impact on suicide rates. Conditions such as depression, bipolar disorder, schizophrenia-spectrum disorders, substance use disorders, epilepsy, and traumatic brain injury significantly increase the risk of suicide, elevating the odds by over 3-fold. While risk factors for suicide are well-established, the same level of certainty does not apply to suicidal ideation. However, the results of a 2017 meta-analysis found that suicidal ideation was the third most robust predictor of future suicide deaths, following prior psychiatric hospitalizations and suicide attempts. Additionally, the most prominent risk factor for future suicidal ideation episodes was past suicidal ideation, underscoring the persistent nature.

retrieved_docs.title: Suicidal Ideation -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Risk factors: The presence of certain risk factors can worsen the prognosis. These include a history of previous suicide attempts, substance abuse, a lack of a support system, chronic physical illness, and concurrent psychiatric conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: Suicidal Ideation -- History and Physical -- Psychiatric Evaluation in Suicide Risk Assessment
retrieved_docs.content: In assessing suicide risk, conducting a thorough psychiatric evaluation encompasses a range of factors. This includes current suicidal ideation, history of suicide attempts, existing psychiatric conditions (such as depression, schizophrenia, substance use disorders, anxiety, borderline personality disorders, and adjustment disorders), psychiatric symptoms, previous psychiatric hospitalizations, and recent biopsychosocial stressors. Additionally, evaluating access to firearms and protective factors, especially those related to reasons for living, is crucial despite limited evidence supporting their impact.

retrieved_docs.title: Washington State Suicide Prevention and Awareness -- Introduction -- Other Risk Factors for Suicide
retrieved_docs.content: Over the years, several other factors have been identified that increases the risk of suicide, and they include: Major childhood adverse events, for example, sexual abuse Discriminated for being gay, lesbian, transgender, or bisexual Having access to lethal means A long history of being bullied Chronic sleep problems

retrieved_docs.title: Suicide: Assessment and Management -- History and Physical -- Psychiatric Suicide Risk Assessment and Formulation
retrieved_docs.content: One helpful way to conceptualize the risk is in terms of the time frame and whether the risk factors are clinically modifiable. Part of medical decision-making for disposition is based on timing. A psychiatrist consulted in an emergency department setting for a patient presenting with thoughts of suicide will evaluate foreseeable risk that spans hours to days, rarely spanning weeks, and seldom months to years. This is in comparison with a psychiatrist who treats a patient with an evidence-based therapy for treatment-resistant depression. The psychiatrist may intend such treatment to mitigate longer-term suicide risk. The psychiatrist evaluates risk factors that can be changed through clinical intervention, known as dynamic risk factors. Historical risk factors for suicide, known as static risk factors, are typically demographic and are not clinically modifiable. However, certain contexts can change static risk factors into dynamic risk factors. For example, an individual who has been divorced for several years carries their divorced status as a static risk factor. However, for example, someone who learned hours ago that their spouse is divorcing them acquires a dynamic risk factor for suicide based on temporal context. The infinite variability of time frames and context of risk influences the interventions regarding the level of treatment, mitigation of modifiable risk factors, and strengthening of protective factors.

Emotional manifestations include emotional lability (“التقلقل الوجداني”), guilt (“الشعور بالذنب”), emotional poverty (“الفقر العاطفي”), indifference (“اللامبالاة”), heightened vigilance (“اليقظة”), and mood swings (“تقلب الحالة المزاجية”). retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2279
retrieved_docs.content: Depression (excluding suicide and self‑harm) (SMQ) is presented as a broad clinical category that includes several distinct subtypes and related conditions. Within this category, reactive depression (اكتئاب تفاعلي), endogenous depression (اكتئاب داخلي المنشأ), melancholic depression (اكتئاب سوداوي) and habituation (الاعتياد) are identified as specific forms of depressive illness. The same overarching depression concept also encompasses postpartum depression (excluding psychosis) (الاكتئاب التالي للولادة (باستثناء الذُهان)), emotional poverty (الفقر العاطفي), heightened vigilance (اليقظة), alcohol poisoning (تسمم كحولي), marijuana use (تعاطي المارجوانا), mood swings (تقلب الحالة المزاجية), memory impairment (خلل في الذاكرة), psychological distress (ضائقة نفسية) and loss of ambition (فقدان الطموح). By grouping these diverse depressive presentations, substance‑related effects, and psychosocial states under a single classification, clinicians can better recognize the varied manifestations of depression, understand their interrelationships, and tailor assessment and treatment strategies to address the full spectrum of depressive disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 662
retrieved_docs.content: Depression (excluding suicide and self‑harm) (SMQ) is presented as an overarching clinical category that encompasses a wide range of specific depressive subtypes, related conditions, and associated phenomena. Within this category, reactive depression (اكتئاب تفاعلي), endogenous depression (اكتئاب داخلي المنشأ), melancholic depression (اكتئاب سوداوي) and habituation (الاعتياد) are identified as distinct members, reflecting different etiological and symptomatic profiles. Post‑partum depression (الاكتئاب التالي للولادة (باستثناء الذُهان)) is also classified under the same SMQ, highlighting its relevance as a specific temporal form of depressive illness. Therapeutic interventions such as electroconvulsive therapy (العلاج بالتخليج الكهربي) are linked to the SMQ, indicating their use in managing severe depressive states. A variety of psychosocial and neurobehavioral factors are likewise incorporated, including emotional poverty (الفقر العاطفي), heightened vigilance (اليقظة), alcohol poisoning (تسمم كحولي), marijuana use (تعاطي المارجوانا), mood swings (تقلب الحالة المزاجية), memory impairment (خلل في الذاكرة), psychological distress (ضائقة نفسية) and loss of ambition (فقدان الطموح). Together, these entities illustrate the complex, multifactorial nature of depression as captured by the SMQ framework, emphasizing the importance of recognizing diverse presentations and contributing factors in clinical assessment and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 435
retrieved_docs.content: The classification “الذهان والاضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ)” is presented as a broad grouping that encompasses a variety of psychotic and affective conditions. Within this group are several specific entities: “ذُهان الطفولة غير المحدد، حالة راهنة أو نشطة” (unspecified childhood psychosis, current or active), “هوس شَبَقِيّ” (schizotypal mania), and “عاطفة متقلصة” (blunted affect). In addition to primary psychotic disorders, the SMQ grouping also includes mood‑related diagnoses such as “اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة راجعة” (major depressive disorder, recurrent episode), “اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة” (major depressive disorder, single episode), “اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة في هدأة تامة” (major depressive disorder, single episode in full remission), “اكتئاب أَوبِيّ” (atypical depression), and “التقلقل الوجداني” (affective lability). This reflects an integrated view in which depressive disorders and affective symptoms are considered part of the broader psychosis spectrum, especially when they may present with psychotic features. The entity “ذُهان الطفولة غير المحدد، حالة راهنة أو نشطة” is also linked to the more general category “disorders usually diagnosed in infancy,” indicating that this form of childhood psychosis is typically identified early in life. Furthermore, “هوس شَبَقِيّ” is related to “ذُهان عضوي” (organic psychosis) through a broader‑relationship link, suggesting that schizotypal mania can be viewed as a subtype or manifestation of an organic psychotic process. Overall, the SMQ framework integrates early‑onset psychotic conditions, organic psychoses, specific manic presentations, and a range of depressive and affective disorders, highlighting the clinical overlap among psychotic and mood disturbances and underscoring the importance of considering these interrelationships in diagnosis and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 47
retrieved_docs.content: Depression (excluding suicide and self‑harm) (SMQ) is presented as a broad clinical category that encompasses multiple depressive subtypes—including reactive depression, endogenous depression, melancholic depression, postpartum depression, and tolerance—as well as distinct sleep‑wake disturbances such as transient disorders of awakening initiation or maintenance and persistent disorders of awakening initiation or maintenance. The SMQ also subsumes a wide range of associated phenomena and comorbid conditions, including emotional poverty, indifference, heightened vigilance, alcohol poisoning, marijuana use, mood swings, attention disturbances, memory disturbances, psychological distress, poor concentration, motor hyperactivity, muscular hyperactivity, and excessive daytime sleepiness. Within this framework, loss of ambition is classified under depression, indicating that diminished motivation may manifest as depressive symptomatology. Non‑infectious encephalitis (SMQ) incorporates both the transient and persistent disorders of awakening initiation or maintenance, linking sleep‑wake regulation abnormalities to an encephalopathic condition. This SMQ further includes hyperkinetic motor activity, hyperactive muscle tone, and primary excessive daytime sleepiness, highlighting a spectrum of motor and arousal disturbances. Apathy is identified as a component of delirium/non‑infectious encephalopathy (SMQ), connecting reduced motivation with acute brain dysfunction. Delirium/non‑infectious encephalopathy also comprises attention disorder, reduced concentration, and primary excessive daytime sleepiness, underscoring the cognitive, attentional, and vigilance impairments that frequently accompany this syndrome. Together, these relationships illustrate the heterogeneous nature of depressive disorders and their overlap with sleep‑wake, motor, and encephalopathic abnormalities, guiding clinicians toward comprehensive assessment and tailored treatment strategies.

Disorders typically diagnosed in infancy are related to ذُهان الطفولة غير المحدد، حالة راهنة أو نشطة, indicating that active unspecified childhood psychosis falls within the early‑life diagnostic spectrum. Collectively, these hierarchical classifications and shared symptomatology provide a comprehensive framework for assessing, diagnosing, and delivering targeted care across organic psychosis, depressive disorders, behavioral dysregulation, encephalitic conditions, and substance‑use disorders, while highlighting the clinical relevance of attention deficits, daytime sleepiness, and the overlap between organic psychosis and schizophrenic mania.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2677
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety disorders serve as a classification for حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة, which are transient organic psychotic conditions. This relationship indicates that brief, organic‑based psychotic episodes are considered within the broader category of anxiety disorders, highlighting an overlap between anxiety symptomatology and short‑lasting psychotic states. Recognizing that حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة fall under anxiety disorders can help clinicians appropriately assess and manage patients who present with transient psychotic features in the context of anxiety‑related pathology.
